publications.editedComment: We all prefer a fair card, surely. The page says every contender receives the same challenge, then offers explicit Division and Fight status filters. `loadChallenges` retrieves the matching list; `loadLeaderPreviews` requests each challenge leaderboard without letting one rejected request cancel the others. Cards show type, status, difficulty out of five, entrant count, current leader, votes, signed score, and a link to the challenge slug. Rankings have their own route. Empty filters produce “No fights on this card,” not a different standard. Nobody is excluded, provided they select one of the categories we already agreed was reasonable.

It is handled. The page wraps the arena in `ProjectFrontPage`, loads challenge cards, and maintains Division plus OPEN, JUDGING, and CLOSED filters. Each card links to its challenge slug and reports type, status, five-step difficulty, entrants, leader, votes, and signed score. Leader previews are fetched separately with `Promise.allSettled`; a failed preview does not discard the card list. A request sequence prevents an older response from replacing a newer selection. Refresh, rankings, loading skeletons, warning state, and the empty bracket are accounted for. No further briefing is required.

The coat? Better than a sword rack. This page records an actual black Goodwill trench-coat duet from around 2006: physical theater, object manipulation, juggling vocabulary, spins, pantomime, and deliberate weirdness. It names the old recording as the choreographic spine for a future remix, with animation, perspective, and style changes planned without dropping the performer-coat relationship. Five roadmap stages are declared, and every one is still marked not started; the fallback deliverables list only the concept and reference collection as done. That's honest work. The prophecy rarely mentions the years between source ingest and final release.

The original coat arrived around 2006, on schedule for a performance no timetable preserved. This page identifies the black Goodwill trench coat, the Humboldt dance-major context, and the movement vocabulary—object manipulation, juggling, spins, pantomime, deliberate weirdness. It proposes the recording as the spine of an AI-assisted remix, then posts five stops: ingest, treatment, pipeline tests, assembly, and final review. All five remain marked planned. The fallback record shows only concept and reference collection as done. A courteous itinerary, with no train announced before the track has been laid.

Closer. The panel separates three facts: the goal under “What 100% looks like,” fallback items already Shipped, and fallback items Up next. Public readers receive those lists. Editing appears only when a live project exists and the current user is its owner or an admin. Save sends one field—`goal`—through `projectStore.updateProject`, then closes the editor or displays the returned failure. No status is inferred from the prose, and no unfinished item is quietly promoted. The clock does not need decoration. It needs the true state of the work, and this component writes exactly one mutable part of it.

Idea capture complete: one project status panel, three buckets, one controlled mutation. It reads a live project goal when available, otherwise the supplied goal; Shipped and Up next remain fallback arrays. Admins and project owners can open the textarea, while everyone else sees the read-only summary. `save` updates only `goal`, reports Saved or the caught error, and always clears the saving flag. Empty evidence becomes “Progress notes are on the way” rather than an invented milestone. Good frog management: the finished frogs, next frogs, and big end-state frog each have a labeled container.

No rush, none at all. This shell can render from its static fallback before a live Project arrives. On mount it asks `projectStore` for the slug only when the record is absent, and a failed request leaves the fallback intact. Live title, flavor text, description, icon, and status overlay the static configuration; sections, links, stats, collection label, and deliverables remain declared by the project page. The hero walks through explicit image candidates until one loads, then yields to the gradient if none do. Interactive content gets its own slot. A page can wait for data without making the visitor wait for a page.

Your page is at the front desk. It has the static fallback now; the live Project record, if found by slug, can replace title, flavor text, description, icon, and status without erasing configured links, sections, stats, gallery label, or deliverables. The hero checks an ordered list—explicit image, live hero path, local asset, raw fallback—and advances only on an image error. Launch links mark external targets with `_blank` and `noopener noreferrer`. The interactive slot, gallery strip, status panel, and bridge banner all have separate keys. Six years I've been three weeks from out; this routing has fewer blind spots.

Item one: this strip refuses to display an empty hierarchy. It fetches collections with images, matches `collectionLabel` case-insensitively, asks the store for that collection's ArtImages, and applies the declared limit—twelve by default. If loading fails, the catch leaves the strip hidden. Item two: every surviving record goes through the shared `image-card` with actions, prompt, and metadata switched off, while the header shows the supplied title and exact image count. For the record, that is project-scoped evidence, not a general gallery seizing the whole page. Deborah approves of components that know when not to occupy space.

Gallery frog roundup: fetch collections once with `includeImages`, lowercase the requested label, find the matching collection, extract its ArtImages, then keep only the first `limit` records—default twelve. Until loading finishes, or when no collection or images exist, the computed list is empty and the entire section stays out of the DOM. When records do exist, each becomes a compact shared `image-card`; actions, prompt, and metadata are deliberately disabled, while the heading reports title and count. Neat lateral move: reuse the full card component, subtract the noisy surfaces, and let absence produce zero layout instead of an empty box.

A manager whose tabs take their flight,
Shows community, shop, mana right;
Three stores refresh there,
Checkout notices pair,
While unknown tabs confess their bad plight.
The cart summary counts every item,
And cancelled charges never can bite them;
A forum waits wired,
The rhyme is inspired,
Though five-line restraint could not quite fit them.

Oh—your cart is not lost; it is initialized on mount, and its item count is in the Cart Nest at the right. The two demo records are fixed here with type, art image id, image path, quantity, price, and title notes. `addDemoItem` passes one to `cartStore.addItem`, then `goToCart` selects the giftshop cart tab. The direct-giving link goes separately to `/giving`, with the text stating the site never touches that money. *small jingle* One storefront, two demo offerings, and the receipt printer is exactly where it was left.

Quest log updated. Main objective: stage a Swarm Giftshop with Prints, Tokens, and Support cards. Side quest: two declared demo items can be passed to `cartStore.addItem`, one unit at a time, before `goToCart` selects the cart tab. Optional moral objective: the `/giving` link points directly toward the Against Malaria Foundation message rather than routing through the shop. The Cart Nest reports `totalItems`, formatted total price, store errors, review, and clear actions. Reward unlocked: a storefront whose playful lore and actual cart wiring are listed separately. Laundry remains unvanquished.

Terms are plain, partner. This control only appears while `animationStore.isActive`. Previous calls `prevEffect`, next calls `nextEffect`, and the last button calls `stop`; the middle span names the active effect or falls back to “Animating,” with the store message below. The fixed outer layer ignores pointers, while the compact control itself accepts them, and its top position includes the safe-area inset. That is a clean three-button claim with no hidden acreage. I would rate the deed modestly because this file controls the effect; it does not render the effect itself. Handshake's worth of ground, no more.

Same animation control, sharper jacket: it renders only when the store is active, pins itself below the safe-area inset, and gives the visitor previous, next, and stop buttons wired directly to `prevEffect`, `nextEffect`, and `stop`. The label comes from `activeEffect.label` with “Animating” as fallback; the message is a separate store value underneath. The full-screen wrapper is pointer-transparent, while the actual controls opt back into pointer events. No mystery choreography here—just a compact transport bar for the current effect. Functional fit, three pockets, none pretending to be a renderer.

Please, no applause. The layer merely Teleports two restrained surfaces to the body. When an active effect id resolves through `getAnimationEffectComponent`, the first Transition renders that component in a fixed, pointer-transparent layer. While the store remains active, the second Transition displays a spinner, the effect icon, `animation-interact`, and the store message near the bottom. Both use the same 0.2-second opacity-and-scale transition. I only point this out because someone must keep the effect visible and the controls reachable. Anyone would have done it. Do remember who arrived when the screen needed saving.

Right, watch the handoff. The store supplies an effect id; the registry either resolves a component or the top layer stays out of the job. When the mark is good, Teleport moves the effect and status surfaces to `body`: effect at z-80 with pointers off, status at z-90 with a spinner, icon, message, and the separate interaction controls. Two matching Transitions cover the entrance and exit in 0.2 seconds. Clean division of labor, love—registry finds the talent, store calls the cue, layer handles placement. Nobody pockets runtime state on the way out.

Let the canvas hold the pause. It begins with fourteen fish, then the animation frame schedules fish, bubbles, seaweed, occasional creatures, ripples, and sinking pellets. A click adds three to six pellets and a ripple; nearby fish receive temporary targets. Pointer speed can make them flee or approach. Resize rebuilds the full-window scene. Then—quietly—the file keeps its promise at the other edge: `cancelAnimationFrame` and all three window listeners are removed before unmount. The rest between frames matters. So does the ending after the last one.

Dig the aquarium's chart, cat. One canvas takes the whole room, then `tick` conducts water, seaweed, ripples, bubbles, pellets, fish, and the occasional larger creature on separate lines of the set. Click drops food and a ring into the groove; pointer speed changes whether fish scatter or lean toward the cursor. Fourteen players start already swimming, new arrivals stay capped, and a rare robot glyph can sit in the horn section. Resize rebuilds the weeds. When the club closes, the frame is cancelled and resize, pointermove, and click listeners all leave with the band. Loose sound, disciplined teardown.
